options: An adversarial-verification panel for every item
A single sequential loop handling one item at a time
A parallel fan-out with a barrier that waits for all 10,000 results
A pipeline that processes each item through stages with no all-results barrier
explanation: A pipeline (stage per item, no barrier) is the right pattern for high-throughput streams of independent items: each item flows through without waiting for the rest, so a slow item does not stall the others. A barrier fan-out suits cases that genuinely need all results together; a sequential loop or per-item verification panel would not sustain the throughput cost-effectively.

question: An orchestrator agent delegates a research task to a worker subagent. The worker keeps calling its "search" tool over and over, never producing a final answer, and the session burns tokens until it crashes. What is the most direct cause of this non-termination?
options: The worker has no explicit success criteria or max-iteration cap, so the loop has no defined stopping condition
Prompt caching was not enabled on the worker
The orchestrator used hub-and-spoke instead of a pipeline pattern
The worker is running on Haiku instead of Opus
explanation: Agentic loops terminate only when they have an explicit success criterion and a hard bound such as a max-iteration cap or token/cost budget. A worker that keeps searching without either will loop until it exhausts context or budget. This is the canonical infinite-loop failure mode and the fix is to define "done" and cap iterations.

question: A long-running agent session begins returning errors and truncated responses after roughly an hour of continuous tool calls and observations. Token usage per turn keeps climbing. What is happening?
options: The API key is rate-limited after an hour
The context window is being exhausted as accumulated tool results and history fill it
The model's weights are being corrupted by repeated calls
Temperature drifted upward over the session
explanation: Every tool call and observation appended to the conversation consumes context. Over a long session this monotonically grows until the window is exhausted, producing truncation and errors. The remedy is compaction/summarization of older history, offloading detail to subagents with isolated context, or pruning stale tool results.

question: Claude consistently calls a "get_user_profile" tool when the user actually wants order history, even though a "get_order_history" tool exists. What is the single best fix?
options: Set tool_choice to "any" so the model is forced to call a tool
Lower max_tokens so responses are shorter
Sharpen both tool descriptions to state precisely what each does and when NOT to use it
Switch the API to streaming mode
explanation: Wrong-tool selection is almost always caused by vague or overlapping tool descriptions. The most effective remedy is to make each description clearly state what the tool does, when to use it, and when not to, so the boundaries between tools are unambiguous. Keeping the tool set small and distinct compounds the benefit.

question: In a very long document-analysis prompt, Claude reliably uses facts from the first and last pages but keeps ignoring a critical constraint placed in the middle. What is the phenomenon and the fix?
options: Token starvation; increase max_tokens
Cache eviction; shorten the TTL
Tool misfire; add a verification tool
Lost-in-the-middle; move the critical constraint to the start or end of the context
explanation: The lost-in-the-middle effect describes models attending less to information buried in the middle of a long context. The standard mitigation is to place the most important instructions and data at the start and end, and to use clear structure such as XML tags. Repeating a critical constraint near the end is a common reinforcement tactic.

question: A parallel fan-out spawns 12 subagents to summarize 12 sections, each running Opus, and monthly costs triple. Quality requirements for these narrow summaries are modest. Which change most reduces cost while preserving the pattern?
options: Run the narrow subagents on Haiku and reserve Opus for the orchestrator's synthesis
Disable streaming on all subagents
Serialize the subagents into a pipeline
Increase each subagent's max_tokens
explanation: Parallel fan-out multiplies token usage by the number of subagents, so running every one on the most expensive model inflates cost. Matching model to role, using Haiku for narrow summarization tasks and reserving Opus for the harder synthesis step, is the standard cost-control move while keeping the parallel pattern intact.

question: A coding agent's edits keep breaking a project invariant (never edit files under infra/). Telling the agent in chat each time has not stopped it on later sessions. What is the most durable systemic fix?
options: Ask the user to remind the agent at the start of each session
Encode the invariant as a hard rule in CLAUDE.md and enforce it with a PreToolUse hook that blocks edits to infra/
Lower the temperature to 0
Switch to a larger context window
explanation: Invariants should be enforced centrally rather than hoping each agent behaves. A hard rule in CLAUDE.md states the policy, and a PreToolUse hook can observe and block any tool call that would edit a forbidden path. Together they make the guardrail systemic and session-independent.

question: An agent occasionally calls a non-idempotent "charge_card" tool twice during a retry, double-charging customers. What is the single best fix?
options: Add retry-with-backoff logic that always re-issues the same call
Increase the model temperature to add variety
Make the tool idempotent with an idempotency key so repeated calls with the same key have no extra effect
Increase the max-iteration cap so the agent has more attempts
explanation: Side-effecting tools in agentic loops must be idempotent because retries and re-planning can re-issue the same action. Passing an idempotency key and having the server reject duplicates guarantees at-most-once effect. Naive retry without idempotency is the root cause, not a fix.

question: A pipeline of subagents produces inconsistent JSON: sometimes valid, sometimes prose wrapped around JSON, breaking the downstream parser. What is the most reliable way to force well-formed structured output?
options: Use streaming so partial JSON arrives sooner
Raise the temperature so outputs vary more
Ask politely in the prompt for "only JSON, no prose"
Define a tool with an input_schema and have the model return the structured data as a tool call
explanation: Forcing JSON through tool use with a defined input_schema is the most reliable structured-output technique because the model must emit data matching the schema. Prefill and explicit format instructions help but are weaker. Always validate the result against the schema downstream.

question: A multi-turn agent gives a correct answer about half the time and a wrong one the other half for the same input, with temperature at 1.0 and no examples. Which two adjustments most improve consistency? (Choose two.)
options: Add a few well-chosen few-shot examples of the desired output
Switch transport from stdio to HTTP
Enable prompt caching
Lower the temperature toward 0 for deterministic, focused outputs
explanation: Flaky outputs on identical inputs are driven by high sampling temperature and a lack of demonstrated format. Lowering temperature reduces variance, and few-shot examples anchor the desired output shape and content. Together they sharply improve consistency.

question: A subagent finishes its task and its detailed working notes are expected to appear in the user's chat, but the user only sees the orchestrator's summary. Is this a bug?
options: No; a subagent returns only its final message to the caller, and that result is not shown directly to the user
Yes; subagent transcripts should always stream to the user
Yes; the hook swallowed the output
No; the user must enable verbose mode to see it
explanation: Subagents run with their own isolated context and return only their final message to the caller. That result flows to the orchestrator and is not shown directly to the user. Seeing only the orchestrator's synthesis is expected behavior, not a defect.

question: An agent stops too early, declaring success after one tool call when the task clearly needed several steps. What design change best addresses premature stopping?
options: Remove the max-iteration cap entirely
Define explicit, verifiable success criteria and add a verification/critic step before the agent declares done
Disable tool use after the first call
Increase temperature to encourage exploration
explanation: Premature stopping is the opposite failure of infinite looping and is fixed by making "done" explicit and checkable. Clear success criteria tell the agent what completion requires, and a verification or critic step confirms the goal is met before stopping. This complements, rather than replaces, an iteration cap.

question: A team marked a cache_control breakpoint after a 30-token greeting but before the 8,000-token tool definitions and reference docs. They see almost no cache savings. What went wrong?
options: The 1-hour TTL is too short
Tool definitions cannot be cached
They placed the breakpoint too early, so the large reusable content sits outside the cached prefix
Cache writes are free so there is nothing to save
explanation: Prompt caching covers the prefix up to the cache_control breakpoint. To save the most, the breakpoint must come after the large stable content such as tool definitions and reference documents. Placing it before that bulk leaves the expensive reusable tokens uncached.

question: Claude Code keeps prompting for permission on routine read-only commands, slowing an automated review. Where is the correct place to allow those commands?
options: Disable hooks
Set the model temperature to 0
Hardcode approvals into the CLAUDE.md memory file
Add an allow rule for those commands in settings.json under the permission configuration
explanation: Allowed tools and commands plus permission modes are configured in settings.json. Allowlisting routine read-only commands there stops repeated prompts without weakening guardrails on sensitive actions. CLAUDE.md is for memory and conventions, not permissions.

question: A headless Claude Code run in CI hangs waiting for interactive input and the pipeline times out. What is the fix?
options: Run in non-interactive mode using the -p/--print flag suited for pipelines
Increase the context window to 1M
Add more CLAUDE.md context
Switch to stdio transport
explanation: Claude Code supports a non-interactive headless mode via -p/--print designed for pipelines and CI. Running interactively in CI causes the process to block on prompts and time out. The print mode produces output without requiring interactive input.

question: Two MCP servers each expose a tool named "search" with near-identical descriptions, and Claude unpredictably picks the wrong one. What most directly reduces the wrong-tool selection?
options: Set tool_choice to "none" globally
Give each tool a distinct name and a description stating what it searches and when to use it instead of the other
Raise temperature to let the model explore tools
Increase max_tokens so the model can describe its choice
explanation: Overlapping, vaguely described tools are the leading cause of wrong-tool selection. The most direct remedy is distinct names and descriptions that say what each tool searches and when to use it rather than the other, removing the ambiguity. tool_choice, temperature, and output length do not address description quality.

question: An orchestrator's context fills up because every subagent's full intermediate transcript is appended to the lead agent's history. What property of the hub-and-spoke pattern is being violated?
options: Fan-out should be unbounded
The orchestrator must run on Haiku
Subagents should keep their own isolated context and return only their result to the orchestrator
Subagents must share one global context
explanation: In hub-and-spoke orchestration, each subagent has its own isolated context and returns only its final result, keeping the orchestrator's context clean. Appending full subagent transcripts back into the lead defeats this and exhausts context. Pass summaries/results, not raw transcripts.

question: A prompt buries the actual task in the middle of 40 pasted log files with no structure. Output quality is poor and inconsistent. Which two prompt-engineering fixes apply best? (Choose two.)
options: State the task instruction at the start and restate it at the end
Increase max_tokens
Wrap sections in XML tags to give the model clear structure
Disable streaming
explanation: Poor structure plus a mid-buried instruction triggers lost-in-the-middle and inconsistent results. Using XML tags to delineate sections and placing the task instruction at the start and end (where attention is strongest) directly address both problems. Output length and streaming are irrelevant here.

question: During an agentic loop, one tool returns a malformed result and the agent retries the same broken call repeatedly, each failure compounding into more retries. What is this failure mode and a good mitigation?
options: Lost-in-the-middle; move the tool description to the end
Cache miss; enable prompt caching
Premature stopping; remove the success criteria
Error cascade; add bounded retries, error handling, and an iteration cap so failures cannot compound indefinitely
explanation: An error cascade occurs when a failing tool call triggers retries that themselves fail and multiply. Mitigations include bounding retries (with backoff), handling errors gracefully so the agent can change course, and capping iterations so a cascade cannot run unbounded. This is distinct from attention or caching issues.

question: A user reports that a project-root CLAUDE.md rule is being ignored inside one subdirectory where a nested CLAUDE.md exists with a conflicting instruction. What explains the behavior?
options: Deeper, more-specific CLAUDE.md files extend and can override higher-level ones for files in their scope
Nested CLAUDE.md files are never loaded
CLAUDE.md cannot contain rules, only prose
Only the user-global CLAUDE.md is ever applied
explanation: CLAUDE.md is hierarchical: user-global, project-root, and nested per-directory files all load, with deeper/more-specific files extending and able to override higher ones. A conflicting nested file therefore wins inside its directory. This is expected precedence, not a bug.

question: An MCP integration tries to let the model decide when to pull specific database rows into context, but rows are exposed as MCP Resources and the model never includes them. Why?
options: Resources can only be used over stdio
Resources are application-controlled context the host chooses to include, not model-invoked, so the model cannot pull them on its own
Resources are user-controlled like slash commands
Resources must be wrapped in a Tool to load
explanation: MCP exposes Tools (model-controlled), Resources (application-controlled), and Prompts (user-controlled). Resources are context the host application chooses to include by URI; the model does not invoke them. To let the model decide when to fetch rows, expose that capability as a Tool instead.

question: A reviewer wants Claude to surface its reasoning before committing to a verdict on a tricky compliance question, but currently it answers instantly and sometimes wrong. Which two techniques fit? (Choose two.)
options: Prompt it to think step by step (chain-of-thought) before the final answer
Set tool_choice to "none"
Enable extended thinking so it reasons internally before answering
Reduce max_tokens to force brevity
explanation: For complex reasoning, prompting chain-of-thought ("think step by step") or enabling extended thinking gives the model space to reason before committing to an answer, improving accuracy on hard judgments. Limiting output or disabling tools does the opposite or is irrelevant.

question: An agent that must call exactly one of three tools on each turn sometimes answers in prose without calling any tool. Which setting most directly enforces a tool call?
options: Increase temperature
Set tool_choice to "none"
Set tool_choice to "any" so the model must call some tool
Set tool_choice to "auto"
explanation: tool_choice controls invocation: "auto" lets the model decide (allowing prose-only turns), "any" forces some tool call, "tool" forces a specific one, and "none" forbids tools. To guarantee a tool is called each turn, "any" is the direct lever.

question: A loop-until-dry batch process is supposed to stop after several empty rounds but never stops because new no-op rounds keep being treated as progress. What is the most likely defect?
options: The pattern should have used a barrier
Prompt caching expired mid-loop
The orchestrator is on Sonnet instead of Opus
The empty-round (K consecutive empty results) termination condition is not correctly detected or counted
explanation: The loop-until-dry pattern repeats until K consecutive empty rounds confirm there is nothing left. If empty rounds are not detected or the counter resets on no-op work, the stop condition never fires. Fixing the empty-round detection and counting restores termination.

question: A team's adversarial-verification step is producing false confirmations because the same agent that generated a finding also verifies it. What principle is violated?
options: Verification should be done by an independent skeptic, not the agent that produced the finding
The verifier must share the generator's context
Verification must always run on Haiku
Verification should be skipped to save tokens
explanation: Adversarial verification works because an independent skeptic, ideally with separate context, tries to refute findings. When the generator verifies its own output, it inherits the same blind spots and rubber-stamps errors. Independence is the key property to restore.

question: A skill packaged in .claude/skills/ is meant to be triggerable by the user with a slash command, but typing /my-skill does nothing. What should you check first?
options: That the 1M context window is on
That the skill is correctly installed/named in .claude/skills/ so it is exposed as a user-invocable /skill-name
That streaming is enabled
That temperature is set above 0
explanation: Skills are packaged model-invoked capabilities stored in .claude/skills/, and many are also user-invocable via /skill-name. If the slash command does nothing, the first thing to verify is that the skill is present and correctly named in that directory so it is registered. Model parameters are irrelevant.

question: An agent designed to summarize 200 incoming tickets per minute uses Opus and is both slow and expensive for what is a simple, high-volume task. What model choice fixes the mismatch?
options: Use Message Batches with a 24-hour window
Use a 1M-token context window
Use Haiku for the high-volume, low-latency, low-complexity summarization
Use Opus with extended thinking
explanation: Model choice should match the task. High-volume, low-latency, low-complexity work like ticket summarization is the canonical Haiku use case, giving the lowest cost and fastest responses. Opus is reserved for the hardest reasoning and agentic work.

question: A streaming chat UI shows nothing for 8 seconds then dumps the full answer at once, hurting perceived responsiveness. The backend calls the Messages API without streaming. What is the fix?
options: Enable prompt caching to stream output
Lower temperature to speed generation
Switch to the Message Batches API
Enable streaming via SSE so tokens arrive incrementally for better perceived latency
explanation: For interactive UX, streaming via SSE sends tokens as they are generated so the user sees progress immediately. A non-streaming call returns the whole response at once, producing the dead-then-dump experience. Batches are for non-real-time work and are the wrong tool here.

question: An agent is given 25 tools and frequently selects suboptimal ones or stalls deciding. Beyond improving descriptions, what structural change reduces selection errors?
options: Reduce and consolidate the tool set so it is small and distinct
Raise temperature for more exploration
Add more tools so the agent has options
Force tool_choice to a single tool permanently
explanation: Large tool sets increase the chance of overlap and confusion. Keeping the tool set small and distinct, consolidating redundant capabilities, reduces selection errors and decision stalls. This is a structural complement to writing clear descriptions.

question: A prompt sometimes returns answers in the user's language and sometimes in English, breaking a downstream consumer that expects one format. The prompt gives no examples. What is the most effective fix?
options: Increase temperature for more natural language
Add few-shot examples demonstrating the exact required output format
Switch to the Batches API
Enable extended thinking
explanation: Inconsistent output format on similar inputs is best fixed by demonstrating the desired output with a few well-chosen few-shot examples, which strongly anchor format adherence. Explicit format instructions and, for structure, tool-use schemas reinforce this. Temperature and batching are irrelevant to formatting.

question: After enabling prompt caching, a developer notices the first request of each session costs more than before, then later requests are cheaper. They worry caching is broken. Is it?
options: Yes; cache writes should be free
Yes; reads should cost more than writes
No; the first request pays the higher cache-write cost, and subsequent reads are much cheaper
No; but only because the TTL is one week
explanation: Prompt caching charges a premium on the cache write (roughly 25% over base input) and then much cheaper reads (around 10% of base) on hits within the TTL. So a more expensive first request followed by cheaper subsequent ones is exactly the expected behavior, not a malfunction.

question: An orchestrator fans out work to 8 parallel subagents and then must combine all results, but it sometimes proceeds with only 5 because faster subagents return first. What orchestration element is missing?
options: A loop-until-dry empty-round counter
A pipeline stage per item
An adversarial verifier
A barrier that waits for all subagent results before the orchestrator proceeds
explanation: When a parallel fan-out must combine all results, it needs a barrier to wait until every subagent has returned before synthesizing. Without the barrier the orchestrator races ahead with partial results. This is the parallel-fan-out-with-barrier pattern.

question: A PreToolUse hook is supposed to block dangerous shell commands but commands still run. The hook script exits 0 in all cases. What is wrong?
options: The hook must signal a block (non-zero/blocking response); always exiting 0 lets every call through
The block must be configured in CLAUDE.md
Hooks cannot block tool calls, only observe
Hooks only run on PostToolUse
explanation: Hooks fire on lifecycle events such as PreToolUse and can observe, block, or modify tool calls. To actually block, the hook must emit the blocking signal (e.g., a non-zero exit or blocking response); a script that always exits 0 approves every call. Fix the script to return the block on dangerous commands.

question: A document Q&A system returns confident but unsupported answers. The architect wants answers traceable to the source text. Which feature directly supports grounded, attributable responses?
options: stdio transport for MCP
The Citations feature for grounded answers tied to source spans
Higher temperature for more creative answers
The Batches API for cheaper runs
explanation: Claude's Citations feature produces grounded answers tied to spans of the source material, which is exactly what is needed to make Q&A answers attributable and reduce unsupported claims. Temperature, batching, and transport do not address grounding.

question: An agent must produce a strict JSON object, but with format instructions alone it occasionally adds a markdown code fence or trailing commentary. Besides tool-use schemas, which technique helps constrain the very start of the output?
options: Enable streaming
Use the Batches API
Prefill the assistant turn with the opening of the JSON to constrain the output
Increase temperature
explanation: Prefill seeds the start of the assistant turn, so beginning it with the opening brace of the JSON constrains the model to continue in that structure and suppresses fences or preamble. Combined with tool-use schemas and validation it yields reliable structured output. Temperature and transport do not constrain format.

question: A long agent session degrades in quality as it goes, repeating itself and losing the thread, well before hitting hard context limits. Which technique best preserves useful context over time?
options: Switching transports mid-session
Forcing tool_choice none
Raising temperature to refresh creativity
Periodic compaction/summarization of older history to keep salient context concise
explanation: As agentic sessions grow, accumulated history dilutes salient context and degrades coherence. Periodic compaction or summarization condenses older turns into a concise form, preserving the important context while freeing space. This is the standard long-session reliability technique.

question: A solutions architect notices that two MCP servers were built, one for "customer data" and one for "customer profiles," and their tools overlap and confuse the model. What design guideline was missed?
options: One server per logical capability with clear, non-overlapping tool names
Combine all capabilities into one mega-tool
Expose everything as Prompts
Always run servers over SSE, never stdio
explanation: MCP design guidance is one server per logical capability with clear, distinct tool names, plus resources for read context and prompts for user-triggered workflows. Two overlapping "customer" servers violate this and confuse selection. Consolidating into one well-named capability with distinct tools resolves it.

question: An agent that books travel sometimes calls book_flight twice for one request after the user rephrases. The tool is not idempotent. Which is the best architectural remedy?
options: Set temperature to 1 for variety
Make book_flight idempotent so repeated calls with the same key do not create duplicate bookings
Disable streaming
Increase max-iteration cap so it has more attempts
explanation: Side-effecting tools used inside agentic loops should be idempotent because rephrasings, retries, and re-planning can re-issue the same action. Designing book_flight to dedup on an idempotency key ensures repeated calls do not create duplicate bookings. Iteration caps and sampling settings do not solve this.

question: An evaluation pipeline that scores model outputs against a rubric is being run interactively one request at a time and takes days. There is no latency requirement and volume is huge. Which two changes fit best? (Choose two.)
options: Increase temperature to diversify scores
Enable streaming to speed each request
Move the evals to the Message Batches API for asynchronous bulk processing
Use a cheaper model such as Haiku for narrow scoring where quality allows
explanation: Large, non-interactive eval runs are an ideal Batches workload, gaining a roughly 50% discount and async throughput. Using a cheaper model like Haiku for narrow scoring further reduces cost where quality permits. Streaming and higher temperature do not help bulk eval throughput or reliability.

question: A custom subagent type was added to the registry but the orchestrator never delegates to it. The orchestrator's instructions do not mention when to use it. What is the most likely cause?
options: The 1M context window must be enabled
Custom subagent types cannot be registered
The orchestrator lacks guidance on when to delegate to that subagent type, so it never selects it
Subagents must run on Opus to be selectable
explanation: You can define custom agent types via the registry, but the orchestrator will only delegate to a subagent when its instructions describe when that subagent should be used. Registration alone is insufficient; the lead needs criteria for routing work to it. Adding delegation guidance fixes the omission.

question: An agent's monthly token bill is dominated by a long-running loop that never resets, accumulating thousands of turns. Caps are absent. Which two controls most directly bound runaway cost? (Choose two.)
options: Switching to SSE streaming
A token/cost budget that halts the loop when exceeded
A max-iteration cap on the loop
Adding more tools to the agent
explanation: Runaway cost in agentic loops is bounded by hard limits: a token/cost budget that halts execution when exceeded and a max-iteration cap that limits the number of turns. These are the canonical guardrails against runaway token cost. Streaming and adding tools do not bound spend.

question: A prompt's system message is a wall of text mixing role, examples, raw data, and the question with no delimiters, and the model conflates the data with instructions. What is the most effective structural fix?
options: Use the 1M context window
Increase temperature so it reads more carefully
Send it as a Batches job
Use XML tags to separate role/instructions, examples, context, and the question
explanation: When instructions, examples, and data are intermixed, the model can conflate them. Using XML tags such as separate sections for instructions, examples, context, and the question gives clear structure that Claude attends to well, preventing conflation. This is a core prompt-structuring technique.

question: A pipeline orchestration processes items stage by stage with no need to wait for all items at once, yet the team added a global barrier that idles workers and slows throughput. What should they do?
options: Remove the unnecessary barrier; a pipeline has a stage per item and needs no all-results barrier
Add a second barrier for safety
Convert to adversarial verification
Switch all stages to Opus
explanation: A pipeline processes a stage per item and does not require a barrier, since there is no point at which all results must be combined at once. Adding a global barrier forces synchronization that idles workers and reduces throughput. Removing it restores the pipeline's natural flow.

question: A developer wants Claude Code to enforce that every commit message ends with a specific trailer, automatically, without relying on the model to remember. Which mechanism is designed for this?
options: A few-shot example in the prompt
A hook fired on the relevant lifecycle event to enforce or modify the action
A 1M context window
A higher temperature
explanation: Hooks are shell commands fired on lifecycle events that can observe, block, or modify actions, making them the right deterministic enforcement mechanism rather than hoping the model remembers a convention. A CLAUDE.md rule states intent, but a hook guarantees the behavior.

question: An agent calls a tool and then never continues, appearing to hang after the tool_use block. Investigation shows the app emitted a tool_result with a mismatched tool_use_id. What is the cause?
options: The tool description was too long
The model needs higher temperature to continue
The tool_result must reference the matching tool_use_id; a mismatch breaks the loop continuation
Streaming must be disabled to continue
explanation: In the tool-use flow, the model emits a tool_use block, the app executes and returns a tool_result matched by tool_use_id, then the model continues. If the id does not match the originating tool_use, the model cannot correlate the result and the loop stalls. Returning the correct tool_use_id fixes it.

question: A remote MCP server is being run as a local subprocess piped over stdio, but the team needs to host it as a shared network service for multiple clients. Which transport fits?
options: Batches API transport
stdio, because it scales to many remote clients
No transport is needed for remote servers
Streamable HTTP/SSE for a remote, network-accessible server
explanation: MCP supports two transports: stdio for a local subprocess and streamable HTTP/SSE for remote servers. A shared network service for multiple clients needs the remote HTTP/SSE transport, not stdio. Choosing the right transport for the deployment topology resolves the mismatch.

question: An architect must reduce variance in a Claude judge that scores essays, where the same essay gets different scores across runs. Which two settings or techniques most improve repeatability? (Choose two.)
options: Provide few-shot exemplars of scored essays with rationale
Set temperature low (near 0) for the judge
Run the judge through the Batches API
Enable the 1M context window
explanation: An LLM judge that produces inconsistent scores benefits from low temperature to cut sampling variance and from few-shot scored exemplars that anchor how the rubric is applied. These directly improve repeatability. Batching and window size do not address variance.

question: A nested CLAUDE.md was added in a subdirectory to relax a global hard rule for that area, but the relaxation is not taking effect. The file is named claude.md (lowercase). What is the issue?
options: The memory file must be named CLAUDE.md to be auto-loaded; the lowercase file is not recognized
Nested files can never override global rules
The file must be in .claude/skills/
Global rules cannot be relaxed at all
explanation: CLAUDE.md is the auto-loaded persistent memory file, and the hierarchy lets deeper files extend or override higher ones within their scope. A misnamed lowercase file is not recognized and therefore is not loaded, so its overrides never apply. Naming it CLAUDE.md fixes the problem.

question: An orchestrator spawns subagents that each independently re-derive the same large shared analysis, multiplying token cost. The analysis is identical for all. What is the most efficient fix?
options: Add more subagents to parallelize the duplication
Compute the shared analysis once in the orchestrator and pass the result to subagents
Give each subagent the 1M context window
Raise temperature so results differ
explanation: Parallel subagents multiply token usage, so having each one re-derive the same shared analysis is wasteful. Computing it once in the orchestrator and passing the result down eliminates the duplication while keeping isolated contexts for the unique parts of each subagent's work. This directly controls token economics.

question: A team wants Claude Code to expose a reusable, user-triggered workflow (gathering inputs then running steps) surfaced as a slash command. Which MCP primitive matches this intent?
options: A 1-hour cache TTL
Tools, which are model-controlled actions
Prompts, which are user-controlled reusable templates often surfaced as slash commands
Resources, which are application-controlled context
explanation: MCP Prompts are user-controlled reusable templates, commonly surfaced as slash commands, which exactly matches a user-triggered workflow. Tools are model-controlled and Resources are application-controlled context, so neither fits a user-initiated template. Choosing the right primitive aligns control with intent.

question: A long agentic coding session begins giving answers that contradict earlier established decisions made many turns ago. The early decisions are far up in a now-huge context. Which two mitigations best preserve those decisions? (Choose two.)
options: Summarize and re-surface key decisions near the end of the context periodically
Raise temperature to encourage new ideas
Compact older history into a concise decisions log kept in context
Disable tool use
explanation: As context grows, early decisions get buried (lost-in-the-middle) and diluted. Periodically summarizing and re-surfacing those decisions near the end, and compacting older history into a concise decisions log kept in context, both preserve the commitments where the model will attend to them. Temperature and tool changes do not help.

question: A subagent role is narrowly scoped (extract dates from text) but was assigned Opus, while the orchestrator doing complex multi-step planning was assigned Haiku and produces poor plans. What is the corrective?
options: Use the Batches API for the orchestrator
Use Haiku for both to save money
Use Opus for both to be safe
Swap them: use Haiku for the narrow extraction subagent and Opus for the complex orchestration
explanation: Model choice should match task difficulty. A narrow extraction task fits Haiku, while complex multi-step orchestration and planning is where Opus's stronger reasoning pays off. The system had them reversed, causing both poor plans and unnecessary cost. Swapping corrects both problems.

question: An agent occasionally calls a destructive "delete_records" tool that should require human confirmation, but it sometimes runs without review. Which Claude Code mechanism best gates such actions?
options: Permission modes / settings.json rules that restrict the tool, plus a PreToolUse hook to require confirmation
A larger context window
Higher temperature for caution
A few-shot example showing careful behavior
explanation: Destructive actions should be gated deterministically: permission modes and settings.json rules can restrict which tools/commands are allowed, and a PreToolUse hook can require confirmation or block the call outright. Relying on model behavior alone is unreliable. Central guardrails are the right control.

question: A prompt asks for a one-paragraph answer but the model returns a 2,000-word essay, blowing the output token budget and cost. Which change most directly and reliably bounds the generated length?
options: Raise temperature for more natural phrasing
Set an appropriate max_tokens limit on the response
Switch to stdio transport
Enable prompt caching
explanation: Output length and cost are most directly bounded by setting max_tokens, which caps generation. Explicitly specifying the desired format and length in the prompt also helps guide brevity, but max_tokens is the hard mechanical limit. Caching, transport, and temperature do not control output length.

question: An agentic workflow needs independent skeptics to confirm a set of findings before they are accepted, because self-confirmation has been unreliable. Which two design choices implement this well? (Choose two.)
options: Have multiple verifiers and accept a finding only if it survives their scrutiny (a judge panel)
Skip verification when the generator is confident
Let the generating agent verify its own output to save tokens
Use separate verifier agents with their own isolated context to challenge findings
explanation: Adversarial verification and judge panels rely on independent skeptics, ideally with isolated context, to challenge and confirm findings before acceptance. Using separate verifiers and requiring findings to survive a panel implements this. Self-verification and skipping on confidence reintroduce the unreliability.

question: A long system prompt has the single most important hard rule buried in paragraph 14 of 30, and the model frequently ignores it. Without trimming the prompt, what placement change helps most?
options: Lower temperature to make it obey
Convert the whole prompt to one line
Move the hard rule to the start of the system prompt and restate it at the end
Move it to exactly the middle for balance
explanation: The model attends best to the start and end of a long context, so the most important instructions should go there, not buried mid-prompt. Moving the hard rule to the top and restating it at the end addresses lost-in-the-middle without requiring the prompt to be trimmed. The exact middle is the worst place.

question: A streaming response over SSE intermittently cuts off mid-sentence and the client treats it as complete, producing flaky truncated outputs. Which two robustness measures help most? (Choose two.)
options: Disable the system prompt
Add retries with backoff to re-request on incomplete streams
Handle the stream's completion/stop events and detect incomplete terminations
Raise temperature to finish faster
explanation: Flaky truncated streaming output is a reliability problem: the client must correctly handle stream completion/stop signals to know when output is genuinely done, and use retries with backoff to recover from transient interruptions. Sampling temperature and removing the system prompt do not address stream integrity.

question: An orchestrator delegates to subagents, but to debug it the team wants the subagents' full intermediate reasoning shown to the end user in real time. They are frustrated this is not the default. What is the accurate framing?
options: The orchestrator and subagents always share one visible context
Subagent reasoning is always streamed to users automatically
Subagents cannot return anything to the orchestrator
By design a subagent returns only its final message to the caller and it is not shown to the user; exposing intermediate work requires deliberate added instrumentation
explanation: Subagents run with isolated context and return only their final message to the caller; that result is not shown directly to the user. So full intermediate reasoning is not user-facing by default, and surfacing it for debugging requires deliberately added instrumentation rather than being automatic. This is expected behavior of the pattern.

question: A developer wants reliable JSON from a high-volume Haiku classifier but sees occasional malformed output under load, breaking parsing. Which combination is the most robust? (Choose two.)
options: Remove the schema to simplify
Validate every output against the schema and retry on failure
Force structured output via tool use with an input_schema
Raise temperature to vary the JSON
explanation: The most robust structured-output approach is to force the shape with tool use and an input_schema and then validate every result against the schema, retrying on the rare failure. This combination handles occasional malformed output under load. Raising temperature or dropping the schema undermines reliability.

question: An agent's permission policy allows a broad set of shell commands, and a runaway loop executed an unintended destructive command before the iteration cap triggered. Which two changes best harden against this class of failure? (Choose two.)
options: Raise the iteration cap so the loop finishes
Add a PreToolUse hook to block destructive commands before execution
Tighten the permission policy to allowlist only the necessary commands
Increase temperature for caution
explanation: Hardening against destructive actions combines least-privilege permissions (allowlisting only what is needed) with a PreToolUse hook that blocks dangerous commands before they execute. These central guardrails act regardless of loop behavior. Raising the iteration cap or temperature does not prevent the unintended command.

question: A few-shot prompt for entity extraction includes three examples, but two of them show the label in lowercase and one in Title Case, and the model's output casing is now inconsistent. What is the cause and fix?
options: The few-shot examples disagree on format, so the model mirrors the inconsistency; make all examples use one consistent casing
The temperature is too low; raise it to smooth casing
The context window is too small for three examples
Prompt caching corrupted one example
explanation: Few-shot examples strongly drive output format, so when the examples themselves disagree on casing the model reproduces that inconsistency. The fix is to make every example demonstrate exactly the desired format. Consistent exemplars are essential for reliable format adherence.

question: A pipeline processes 50,000 incoming documents; each is independently summarized and the results are written to separate rows. There is no step that needs every summary before proceeding. Which orchestration pattern is the best fit?
options: Loop-until-dry with K empty rounds
A simple pipeline (stage-per-item, no barrier)
Parallel fan-out with a barrier
Adversarial verification with skeptic agents
explanation: When items are independent and no step requires all results to be present, a per-item pipeline with no barrier is the simplest and cheapest correct choice. A barrier only earns its cost when a downstream step truly needs the complete result set.

question: A research orchestrator must produce a single ranked report that depends on the complete output of all 8 parallel sub-searches. Which pattern is most appropriate?
options: Pipeline with no barrier
Sequential single-agent chain
Parallel fan-out with a barrier before the ranking step
Loop-until-dry
explanation: A downstream aggregation that requires every parallel result is the textbook case for a barrier: fan out the work, then synchronize before the dependent step. Pipelines without barriers risk acting on incomplete data.

question: You are crawling an unbounded backlog of tickets that other systems keep adding to; you want to keep processing until things have clearly settled. Which orchestration pattern fits best?
options: Single pipeline pass
Adversarial judge panel
Fixed parallel fan-out with a barrier
Loop-until-dry, stopping after K consecutive empty rounds
explanation: An unknown, possibly-growing work set calls for loop-until-dry: keep iterating and only stop after several consecutive rounds find nothing new. Fixed fan-out and single passes assume a known finite set.

question: A security review must catch subtle false positives, so you want independent agents to challenge each finding before it is reported. Which pattern is most appropriate?
options: Adversarial verification (independent skeptic agents confirm findings)
Loop-until-dry
Simple pipeline
Hub-and-spoke with no verification
explanation: When the goal is to reduce false positives by having findings independently challenged, adversarial verification with skeptic agents is the designed pattern. It trades extra tokens for higher precision.

question: An orchestrator delegates a large codebase audit to several specialized subagents and wants its own context to stay small. What is the key architectural property that achieves this?
options: The orchestrator shares one context window with all subagents
Each subagent has its own isolated context and returns only its result to the orchestrator
All subagents write to the orchestrator's system prompt
Subagents stream all intermediate tokens back to the orchestrator
explanation: Hub-and-spoke keeps the orchestrator clean because each subagent works in an isolated context and returns only its final result. That isolation is what prevents intermediate work from flooding the lead agent's window.

question: A high-volume classification job runs nightly on 2 million short snippets where per-item reasoning is shallow and latency per item is not critical. Which model and API choice minimizes cost without sacrificing throughput?
options: Opus via streaming Messages API
Sonnet via synchronous Messages API
Haiku via the Message Batches API
Opus via Batches API
explanation: Shallow, high-volume, latency-tolerant work is the ideal case for Haiku plus the Batches API: cheapest model and an additional ~50% discount for asynchronous bulk processing. Opus and synchronous calls waste money here.

question: You need the model to take an action against an external system on its own judgment during a conversation. Which MCP primitive should expose that capability?
options: A settings.json permission
A Prompt (user-controlled template)
A Resource (application-controlled context)
A Tool (model-controlled action)
explanation: MCP Tools are model-controlled actions, so anything the model should decide to invoke belongs as a Tool. Resources are application-controlled read context and Prompts are user-triggered templates.

question: The host application wants to attach specific database rows as read-only background context that the app (not the model) decides to include. Which MCP primitive fits?
options: A Resource exposed by URI
A Tool
A hook
A Prompt
explanation: Application-controlled read context that the host decides to include is the definition of an MCP Resource, addressed by URI. Tools are model-controlled and Prompts are user-controlled, so neither matches.

question: You want to give users a reusable, user-triggered workflow surfaced as a slash command from your MCP server. Which primitive should you use?
options: A subagent
A Prompt
A Tool
A Resource
explanation: MCP Prompts are user-controlled templates often exposed as slash commands, which is exactly a user-triggered reusable workflow. Tools are model-controlled and Resources are application-controlled.

question: Your application must reliably receive a strict JSON object from Claude that conforms to a fixed schema. Which approach is the most reliable per the brief?
options: Use stop_sequences to cut after the first brace
Raise temperature so the model is more creative with JSON
Define a tool with an input_schema and have the model call it
Ask in plain English for JSON and parse the text
explanation: The brief states that forcing JSON via tool use with an input_schema is the most reliable structured-output approach. Prefill and format instructions help but are weaker; raising temperature is counterproductive.

question: A systemic invariant must hold for every agent in your system: no agent may run a destructive shell command. Where is the best place to enforce this?
options: Raising temperature to discourage risky commands
A polite reminder in each agent's user prompt
A note in one subagent's CLAUDE.md only
A PreToolUse hook (and permission policy) that blocks the command centrally
explanation: Systemic invariants should be enforced centrally rather than hoping each agent complies. A PreToolUse hook combined with permission policy can deterministically block disallowed commands across the whole system.

question: A convention applies to the entire monorepo, but one nested package needs a stricter variant of that convention. How should you structure the CLAUDE.md hierarchy?
options: Keep the general rule in the project-root CLAUDE.md and add the stricter variant in that package's nested CLAUDE.md
Put the general rule only in the nested package file
Put every rule, including the package-specific one, only in the root CLAUDE.md
Use the user-global ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md for the package-specific rule
explanation: CLAUDE.md is hierarchical: general rules belong at the root and more-specific, deeper files extend or override them. Placing the stricter variant in the nested package file scopes it correctly.

question: An agentic loop occasionally never terminates because the model keeps calling tools without converging. Which mitigation most directly prevents the infinite loop?
options: Adding more tools
A max-iteration cap on the loop
Switching from Sonnet to Opus
Lowering temperature to 0
explanation: Infinite loops are a known agentic failure mode whose most direct guard is a max-iteration cap. Model swaps and temperature changes do not bound iteration count.

question: You are designing prompt caching for a large reused system prompt plus tool definitions sent on most requests. What is the correct way to think about the cost trade-off?
options: Cache reads cost more than writes, so caching only helps rarely-reused content
Caching changes the output token price
Cache writes cost more (~25% over base) but cache reads are much cheaper (~10% of base), so reuse pays off
Caching only helps if the TTL is one hour
explanation: Prompt caching charges a write premium (~25% over base) once, then serves reads at roughly 10% of base price. For large stable prefixes reused across many requests, that math strongly favors caching.

question: A long agentic session is approaching the context window limit mid-task. Which technique is the appropriate architectural response?
options: Adding more tools to help the model
Increasing max_tokens for the response
Lowering temperature
Compaction/summarization of earlier context
explanation: When a session nears the context limit, compaction or summarization of older content is the designed mitigation. Output-length and sampling settings do not reclaim context space.

question: Critical, must-follow instructions are currently buried in the middle of a very long prompt and the model sometimes ignores them. What does the 'lost in the middle' effect imply you should do?
options: Move the critical instructions to the start and end, and use structure
Shorten every instruction to one word
Raise temperature so the model explores the middle
Duplicate the instructions ten times in the middle
explanation: The lost-in-the-middle effect means information buried mid-context gets less attention. The fix is to place critical instructions at the start and end and use structure such as XML tags.

question: You are deciding how to structure a complex prompt that mixes a long source document, several examples, and task instructions. What is the recommended structuring technique for Claude?
options: Encode everything as base64
Use XML tags to delimit sections such as document, examples, and instructions
Put instructions only in the tool input_schema
Concatenate everything as one unbroken paragraph
explanation: XML tags are the brief's recommended way to structure mixed inputs for Claude, clearly separating documents, examples, and instructions. Unstructured blobs and encodings hurt comprehension.

question: You want the assistant's reply to begin with a specific format and avoid a chatty preamble. Which API feature most directly achieves this?
options: Raise temperature
Increase max_tokens
Prefill the start of the assistant turn
Add more stop_sequences
explanation: Prefilling the assistant turn seeds its first tokens, which is the most direct way to lock the opening format and skip preamble. Token limits, stop sequences, and temperature do not constrain how output begins.

question: You want to run automated code review inside a CI pipeline with no interactive terminal. Which Claude Code capability is designed for this?
options: A Resource primitive
Interactive REPL with manual approvals
A nested CLAUDE.md per directory
Headless / non-interactive mode (-p/--print), e.g. via GitHub Actions
explanation: Claude Code's headless print mode (-p/--print) is designed for non-interactive pipelines and integrates with GitHub Actions for automated reviews and checks. Interactive modes require a human.

question: A repeatable, specialized multi-step workflow (lint, fix, summarize) should be packaged so the model can invoke it and users can also run it as /name. Which Claude Code construct fits?
options: A Skill in .claude/skills/
A hook
A Resource
A permission mode
explanation: Skills package repeatable specialized workflows that the model can invoke and users can also trigger via a slash command. Hooks, resources, and permission modes serve different purposes.

question: Three subagents have overlapping, vaguely-worded tool descriptions and the model frequently picks the wrong tool. According to the brief, what is the single most effective fix?
options: Increase temperature to diversify tool choice
Rewrite tool descriptions to be distinct, stating what each does, when to use it, and when not to
Add a fourth similar tool
Force tool_choice to any
explanation: The brief calls tool description quality the number-one driver of correct tool selection. Distinct descriptions that say what a tool does and when to use or avoid it directly fix wrong-tool selection.

question: You must guarantee the model calls some tool rather than answering from memory, but you do not care which tool. Which tool_choice setting fits?
options: tool (a specific named tool)
auto
any
none
explanation: tool_choice any forces the model to invoke some tool while leaving the choice open. auto may skip tools, none forbids them, and the specific tool setting is too restrictive here.

question: A local MCP server runs as a subprocess on the developer's machine and communicates over stdin/stdout. Which transport is this?
options: WebSocket-only
gRPC
streamable HTTP/SSE
stdio
explanation: MCP defines two transports: stdio for local subprocesses and streamable HTTP/SSE for remote servers. A subprocess over stdin/stdout is stdio.

question: Which of the following are appropriate mitigations for agentic-loop failure modes? (Select all that apply.)
options: Max-iteration caps
Token/cost budgets
Explicit success criteria and verification/critic steps
Removing all error handling to fail fast
explanation: The brief lists max-iteration caps, token/cost budgets, explicit success criteria, verification steps, idempotent tools, and timeouts as loop mitigations. Removing error handling worsens error cascades.

question: A subagent's only job is to extract a date from a short string, called millions of times. Which model best balances the role with token economics?
options: Haiku
Opus
Sonnet
Opus with extended thinking
explanation: The brief recommends choosing cheaper models like Haiku for narrow subagent roles to control token economics. A trivial high-volume extraction does not justify Sonnet or Opus.

question: You want to cap how much a parallel fan-out can cost because parallel subagents multiply token usage. Which two controls most directly address this? (Select all that apply.)
options: Switch the orchestrator to Opus
Set a token/cost budget for the run
Increase max_tokens per subagent
Cap the fan-out (limit the number of parallel subagents)
explanation: Parallel subagents multiply token usage, so capping fan-out and setting a token/cost budget are the direct levers. Raising max_tokens or upgrading the model increases cost.

question: A bulk eval run of 100,000 prompts must complete within a day and cost as little as possible; real-time latency is irrelevant. Which API is the best fit?
options: Synchronous Messages API with streaming
Message Batches API
Synchronous Messages API without streaming
A websocket streaming endpoint
explanation: Large, latency-tolerant batch workloads like evals fit the Message Batches API: a ~24-hour target window with about 50% cost savings. Synchronous and streaming options are for interactive use.

question: For a complex multi-step reasoning task, you want the model to reason before producing a final answer. Which two approaches are aligned with the brief? (Select all that apply.)
options: Chain-of-thought prompting (think step by step)
Extended thinking / reasoning mode
Lowering max_tokens to 5
Forcing tool_choice none and a one-word answer
explanation: Both chain-of-thought prompting and extended thinking are the brief's recommended ways to get reasoning before the answer on complex tasks. Suppressing output or tools blocks reasoning.

question: You are choosing where to encode a hard rule that every developer's Claude Code session in this repo must obey. Which file is the best home?
options: Each developer's ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md
A comment in one source file
The project-root CLAUDE.md
A random nested CLAUDE.md
explanation: Repo-wide hard rules belong in the project-root CLAUDE.md, which is auto-loaded for everyone in the project. User-global and nested files have narrower or personal scope.

question: You want output that matches a few well-chosen examples for tone and format. Which prompt-engineering technique most directly drives that consistency?
options: Raising temperature
Encoding the request in JSON only
Removing the system prompt
Few-shot examples
explanation: Few-shot examples are the brief's recommended lever for driving consistent output tone and format. Raising temperature and removing guidance work against consistency.

question: An orchestrator must split a broad market-research question into independent investigations, run them, and synthesize one answer. Which architecture is most appropriate?
options: Hub-and-spoke orchestrator-worker with isolated subagent contexts
A PostToolUse hook chain
A user-controlled prompt template
A single monolithic agent doing everything in one context
explanation: Breadth tasks that decompose into independent investigations fit hub-and-spoke: the orchestrator delegates to isolated subagents and synthesizes their results. A single context loses parallelism and bloats.

question: You must ground answers in specific source documents and surface verifiable references. Which Claude capability is designed for this?
options: stop_sequences
The Citations feature for grounded answers
tool_choice none
Raising temperature
explanation: The brief lists Citations as the feature for grounded answers backed by sources. Sampling and tool settings do not provide source grounding.

question: A workflow must accept scanned PDFs and photographs as input for analysis. Which Claude capability supports this?
options: Prompt caching
Only plain-text input
Multimodal input (images/vision and PDFs)
stdio transport
explanation: Claude supports multimodal input including images (vision) and PDFs, which covers scanned documents and photographs. Transports and caching are unrelated to input modality.

question: You are designing an MCP server and must decide its scope. What is the recommended design guideline?
options: No tool names so the model infers them
Expose only Resources, never Tools
One giant server exposing every tool in the company
One server per logical capability with clear, distinct tool names
explanation: The brief recommends one server per logical capability with clear, distinct tool names. Monolithic servers and overlapping tools degrade selection and maintainability.

question: A retry strategy is needed for transient API errors and rate limits in a production integration. Which two practices are recommended? (Select all that apply.)
options: Disabling all retries
Retrying immediately in a tight loop forever
Retries with exponential backoff
Idempotency so retries are safe
explanation: Reliable integrations use retries with backoff plus idempotency so repeats are safe. Tight infinite retries and no retries are both poor strategies.

question: You want a guardrail that can observe and potentially block a tool call before it executes in Claude Code. Which lifecycle mechanism is correct?
options: A PreToolUse hook
A few-shot example
A Resource
A PostToolUse hook
explanation: PreToolUse hooks fire before a tool call and can block or modify it, making them the right guardrail point. PostToolUse runs after execution and cannot prevent it.

question: A subagent returns its final message to the orchestrator. Per the brief, who sees that final message by default?
options: No one; it is discarded
The calling orchestrator (it is not shown directly to the user)
Every other subagent
The end user directly
explanation: Per the brief, a subagent's final message returns to the caller (orchestrator) and is not shown directly to the user. This is why hub-and-spoke keeps the user-facing output under orchestrator control.

question: You have a fixed set of 12 independent feature branches to lint in parallel, and you need a consolidated pass/fail report only after all 12 finish. Which pattern fits best?
options: Adversarial verification
Single sequential pipeline
Parallel fan-out with a barrier before the consolidation step
Loop-until-dry
explanation: A known finite set processed in parallel, with a step that needs all results, is fan-out plus a barrier. Loop-until-dry is for unknown queues and sequential pipelines lose parallelism.

question: Which statements about MCP primitive control are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: Tools are model-controlled
Resources are application-controlled
Prompts are user-controlled
Resources are model-controlled actions
explanation: The three MCP primitives map to control owners: Tools are model-controlled, Resources are application-controlled, and Prompts are user-controlled. Calling Resources model-controlled actions is incorrect.

question: You want to constrain a model's output to a strict enum and numeric ranges, validated automatically. What is the most robust design?
options: Truncate output with stop_sequences after the enum
Ask in prose to pick from the enum and trust the text
Use a high temperature for variety
A tool with an input_schema encoding the enum and numeric constraints, then validate
explanation: Encoding enums and numeric ranges in a tool input_schema, then validating the call, is the most robust structured-output design. Prose, high temperature, and truncation do not enforce the schema.

question: You must enforce that a domain invariant holds regardless of which prompt phrasing a user supplies. Where should the invariant live for maximum reliability?
options: Centrally in the system prompt and/or hooks/permission policy
In each user's ad hoc message
In a few-shot example only
In the temperature setting
explanation: Systemic invariants should be enforced centrally in the system prompt, hooks, CLAUDE.md hard rules, or permission policy rather than relying on user phrasing. Examples and sampling settings do not enforce them.

question: You want a single source of truth for which shell commands and tools the agent is allowed to use. Which Claude Code mechanism is designed for this?
options: Raising temperature
settings.json with permission modes
A few-shot example listing allowed commands
A Resource listing commands
explanation: Allowed tools and commands are controlled by settings.json and permission modes, the designed mechanism for tool/command policy. Examples and resources do not enforce it.

question: You want to discourage a model from continuing past a known delimiter in its output. Which parameter is appropriate?
options: temperature
tool_choice
stop_sequences
max_tokens only
explanation: stop_sequences stop generation when a specified string appears, which is the right tool for cutting at a known delimiter. Temperature, tool_choice, and max_tokens do not target delimiters.

question: A nightly job both classifies 500,000 records and must finish by morning at lowest cost, but a small subset of ambiguous records needs deeper reasoning flagged for later. What is the best architecture?
options: Send every record to Opus synchronously
Use Sonnet synchronously for all records
Skip the ambiguous records entirely
Batch the bulk classification with Haiku, and route only flagged ambiguous records to a separate deeper pass
explanation: Tiered routing is the cost-optimal design: cheap Haiku batch for the bulk, with only the small ambiguous subset escalated to deeper reasoning. Sending everything to a costlier model or synchronous calls wastes money.

question: You need a reusable, user-triggered workflow in Claude Code that a developer launches as /deploy-check. Which construct is most appropriate?
options: A slash command (custom command), or a Skill exposed via /name
A PreToolUse hook
A Resource
A higher max_tokens setting
explanation: User-triggered workflows launched as /name are slash commands, including skills surfaced via /skill-name. Resources, hooks, and token settings are not user-launch mechanisms.

question: Your agentic system sometimes stops before the goal is met (premature stopping). Which design element most directly addresses this?
options: Fewer tools
Explicit success criteria the loop checks before terminating
A larger context window
A lower temperature
explanation: Premature stopping is countered by explicit success criteria that the loop must satisfy before terminating. Sampling, tool count, and context size do not define completion.

question: For an agent that must call exactly the get_invoice tool on a billing request, which tool_choice configuration enforces it?
options: none
auto
tool with the specific tool named (force get_invoice)
any
explanation: To force a particular tool, set tool_choice to that specific tool. auto and any do not guarantee the exact tool, and none forbids tools.

question: You want a CLAUDE.md rule to apply to your personal workflow across all repos on your machine. Where should it go?
options: A nested CLAUDE.md in one directory
settings.json permissions
The project-root CLAUDE.md of one repo
The user-global ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md
explanation: Personal, cross-repo preferences belong in the user-global ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md. Project-root and nested files are scoped to specific repos or directories.

question: Which combination best keeps a long-running orchestrator both cheap and reliable? (Select all that apply.)
options: A max-iteration cap and token budget
Prompt caching on the stable system/tool prefix
Cheaper models (Haiku) for narrow subagent roles
Forwarding all subagent intermediate tokens to the orchestrator
explanation: Cost and reliability come from caching stable prefixes, using cheaper models for narrow roles, and capping iterations and budget. Forwarding all intermediate tokens does the opposite by exhausting context.

question: You need structured output but the consumer can tolerate occasional manual fixups and you cannot define a tool for protocol reasons. Which fallback does the brief endorse?
options: Prefill and explicit format instructions, then validate against a schema
Use Batches API to enforce JSON
Just hope the model returns JSON
Raise temperature to encourage structure
explanation: When tool use with input_schema is not possible, the brief endorses prefill and format instructions, validated against a schema, as the next-best structured-output approach. Hoping, raising temperature, and batching do not enforce structure.

question: A system prompt is becoming a dumping ground. Which content most appropriately belongs in the system prompt per prompt-engineering guidance?
options: Raw 500-page logs for every request
Role, instructions, and constraints stated explicitly
User-specific transient chit-chat
The model's temperature value
explanation: The system prompt should clearly state role, instructions, and constraints. Bulk data, API parameters, and transient chat do not belong there.

question: For maximum reliability of correct tool selection across a growing product, which design choice does the brief most strongly recommend?
options: Give all tools nearly identical descriptions
Add as many tools as possible for flexibility
Keep the tool set small and distinct
Hide tool descriptions from the model
explanation: The brief recommends keeping the tool set small and distinct, with clear non-overlapping descriptions, to maximize correct selection. Large overlapping sets and vague descriptions degrade reliability.

question: You are choosing a transport for a remote, hosted MCP server accessed over the network by many clients. Which transport is appropriate?
options: Local file pipe only
No transport; clients connect directly to the model
stdio
Streamable HTTP/SSE
explanation: Remote, hosted MCP servers use the streamable HTTP/SSE transport. stdio is reserved for local subprocess servers.

question: A data-extraction agent must return one of three classification labels with a confidence score, consumed by a strict downstream service. Which design is best?
options: A tool whose input_schema has an enum label and a numeric confidence, validated before use
Free-text 'label: X, confidence: Y' parsed with regex
Stream tokens and stop at the first label word
Ask for prose and let the downstream service interpret it
explanation: A strict downstream contract is best served by a tool input_schema encoding an enum label and numeric confidence, validated before use. Regex over prose and stop-based parsing are brittle.

question: Which are valid uses of prompt caching per the brief? (Select all that apply.)
options: Large reused system prompts
Random per-request unique user text
Reused tool definitions
Reused large documents in the prefix
explanation: Prompt caching pays off for stable, reused prefixes: large system prompts, tool definitions, and documents. Unique per-request text that is never reused does not benefit.

question: A pipeline of independent items occasionally fails on one item and the whole run aborts, cascading failures. Which reliability design best limits the blast radius?
options: Retry the whole batch from scratch endlessly
Isolate per-item failures (idempotent tools, per-item error handling) so one failure does not abort the run
Abort the entire run on the first error
Switch to Opus for all items
explanation: To limit blast radius in an independent-item pipeline, isolate per-item failures with per-item error handling and idempotent tools so one failure does not abort the run. Full aborts and endless retries worsen cascades.

question: You want to choose the cheapest viable model for a moderately complex summarization that runs interactively but not at extreme volume. Which is the most defensible default, and what would push you up or down a tier?
options: Use Batches because it is cheaper
Always start with Haiku regardless of complexity
Start with Sonnet; move to Opus if reasoning quality is insufficient, or Haiku if cost/latency dominates
Always start with Opus regardless of need
explanation: For moderately complex interactive work, Sonnet is the defensible default; escalate to Opus if quality falls short or drop to Haiku if cost and latency dominate. Always-Opus overspends and always-Haiku underserves.

question: Which statements about Claude Code hooks are accurate? (Select all that apply.)
options: They are MCP read-context primitives
They can observe, block, or modify tool calls
They fire on lifecycle events such as PreToolUse and PostToolUse
They are used for guardrails, formatting, and policy
explanation: Hooks fire on lifecycle events like PreToolUse and PostToolUse, can observe, block, or modify tool calls, and are used for guardrails, formatting, and policy. They are not MCP read-context primitives.

question: You want to decide whether to enforce a code-style rule via CLAUDE.md guidance or a PostToolUse formatting hook. The rule must be applied deterministically on every write. Which is the better primary mechanism and why?
options: CLAUDE.md, because guidance is always applied perfectly
A Resource, because it documents the style
Neither; rely on the model's defaults
A PostToolUse hook, because it runs deterministically after each write regardless of model behavior
explanation: Deterministic per-write formatting is best done with a PostToolUse hook that runs the formatter every time, independent of model memory. CLAUDE.md guidance and resources cannot guarantee deterministic application.

question: You must minimize the chance critical safety instructions are ignored in a very long tool-heavy context. Which two techniques together best address this? (Select all that apply.)
options: Lower max_tokens so the model reads faster
Bury them once in the exact middle
Place the safety instructions at the start and end of the context
Use clear structure such as XML tags around them
explanation: To combat lost-in-the-middle, place critical instructions at the start and end and wrap them in clear structure like XML tags. Burying them mid-context or shrinking output budget does not help.

question: A platform team wants Claude Code to run automated code reviews on every pull request inside their GitHub Actions pipeline, with no human at a terminal. Which invocation mode is designed for this?
options: Headless / non-interactive mode using the "-p"/"--print" flag
A custom MCP server that wraps the Claude Code binary
Interactive REPL mode launched normally, scripted with expect
Extended thinking mode toggled on at startup
explanation: Claude Code supports a headless non-interactive mode via the "-p"/"--print" flag, purpose-built for CI/CD, automated reviews, codegen, and checks. It runs to completion and exits without expecting interactive human turns, which is exactly what a GitHub Actions job needs.

question: In a CI pipeline, you invoke "claude -p" to summarize the diff and you need the output as machine-parseable JSON so a later step can gate the merge. What is the most reliable way to get well-formed structured output?
options: Tell the model "only output JSON" and trust the raw text
Ask in the prompt for JSON and additionally constrain the shape via a tool with an input_schema, then parse and validate it
Pipe the natural-language answer through a regex to extract fields
Raise temperature so the model is more creative about formatting
explanation: The spec states structured outputs are most reliably forced via tool use with an input_schema, optionally combined with prefill/format instructions, and then validated against the schema. In a gating pipeline you want deterministic, parseable output, so schema-constrained tool use plus validation is the right design.

question: You are designing a custom MCP server to expose your company's internal incident-management system to Claude. The model should be able to create and resolve incidents on its own initiative when reasoning about an outage. Which MCP primitive should these capabilities be exposed as?
options: Prompts
Resources
Tools
Sampling callbacks
explanation: MCP defines three server primitives: Tools (model-controlled actions), Resources (application-controlled read context by URI), and Prompts (user-controlled templates). Create/resolve incident are actions the model decides to take, so they belong as Tools.

question: A large orchestrator agent fans out to 30 parallel research subagents, and your token spend has exploded. Which combination of mitigations most directly controls cost while preserving the orchestrator pattern? (Select all that apply.)
options: Replace all subagents with one giant single-context agent doing everything serially
Use a cheaper model such as Haiku for the narrow, well-scoped subagent roles
Apply prompt caching to the stable shared prefix the subagents reuse
Cap the fan-out to a smaller number of concurrent subagents
explanation: Parallel subagents multiply token usage, so the levers are: cap fan-out, route narrow roles to cheaper models like Haiku, and use prompt caching on stable reused prefixes. Collapsing everything into a single serial agent abandons the orchestrator-worker pattern rather than tuning it.

question: Your security team requires that Claude Code agents can never run "rm -rf" or push directly to the main branch, regardless of which prompt or subagent is active. What is the most robust place to enforce this invariant across the whole fleet?
options: A polite instruction added to one subagent's prompt
A note in the PR template asking reviewers to watch for it
Lowering the model temperature to make behavior deterministic
A PreToolUse hook (plus permission policy) that inspects and blocks the disallowed commands
explanation: Systemic invariants should be enforced centrally via mechanisms like hooks and permission policy rather than hoping each agent behaves. A PreToolUse hook can observe and block disallowed tool calls deterministically across every agent and prompt, which is exactly what a hard security rule needs.

question: A data team needs to classify 4 million support tickets overnight; latency per ticket is irrelevant but cost matters. Which Anthropic capability best fits?
options: The Message Batches API
Forcing tool_choice to "any" on every request
The 1M-token context window
Streaming via SSE on the Messages API
explanation: The Message Batches API is designed for asynchronous bulk processing with an approximately 24-hour target window and around a 50% cost discount, perfect for non-interactive workloads like bulk classification.
