Streaming addresses interactive latency, which is explicitly not the concern here.

question: You operate a fleet of long-running agentic sessions and want to detect quality regressions before customers do. Which practice is the spec's recommended reliability safeguard?
options: Disabling streaming to simplify the stack
Continuous evals plus observability to catch regressions
Always using Opus regardless of task
Removing all retry logic to fail fast on the first error
explanation: Reliability for production fleets relies on retries with backoff, idempotency, rate-limit handling, streaming for UX, and evals/observability to catch regressions. Of the options, continuous evals plus observability is the safeguard that surfaces quality regressions before users hit them.

question: When designing tool descriptions for a custom MCP server that several agents will share, what most strongly determines whether Claude picks the correct tool?
options: The number of required fields in the input_schema
Whether the tool names use camelCase or snake_case
Description quality: stating what the tool does, when to use it, and when not to
The alphabetical ordering of the tools in the array
explanation: The brief is explicit that tool description quality is the #1 driver of correct tool selection: descriptions must state what the tool does, when to use it, and when NOT to. Overlapping or vague descriptions cause wrong-tool selection, so clarity beats naming or ordering concerns.

question: An orchestrator delegates a focused subtask to a subagent. In the hub-and-spoke pattern, what does the subagent return and to whom?
options: Nothing; the orchestrator must re-derive the answer itself
Its entire intermediate context and tool transcript, streamed to the user
A new system prompt for the orchestrator to adopt
Only its final result, back to the orchestrator (not directly to the end user)
explanation: In the orchestrator-worker (hub-and-spoke) pattern, each subagent runs in its own isolated context and returns only its final message to the orchestrator, not to the end user. This keeps the orchestrator's context clean while still delivering the subagent's result.

question: A monorepo has a root CLAUDE.md with global conventions, and the payments service needs a stricter rule: "never log card numbers." Where should that rule live so it applies to work in that service?
options: A nested CLAUDE.md inside the payments service directory
In a one-off chat message at the start of each session
Only in the user-global ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md
Inside a skill that nobody invokes
explanation: CLAUDE.md is hierarchical: user-global, project-root, and nested per-directory files, where deeper and more-specific files extend and can override higher ones. A payments-specific hard rule belongs in a nested CLAUDE.md within that service directory so it auto-loads for work there.

question: You want independent confirmation that a security-finding agent is not hallucinating vulnerabilities. Which orchestration pattern fits best?
options: Loop-until-dry over the same agent's own output
Adversarial verification with independent skeptic agents that confirm findings
A single pipeline stage with no verification
Raising max_tokens so the agent explains more
explanation: The brief lists adversarial verification, where independent skeptics confirm findings, as an orchestration pattern. Independent verification is the right tool when you need to guard against a single agent hallucinating or over-claiming results.

question: A pipeline runs "claude -p" thousands of times a day, each call prefixed by the same 40-page coding standard and a large tool-definition block. How do you cut cost and latency on the repeated prefix?
options: Disable extended thinking to shrink the prompt
Send the standard as an image instead of text
Mark the stable prefix with a cache_control breakpoint to use prompt caching
Switch every call to multi-select questions
explanation: Prompt caching marks stable prefixes with cache_control breakpoints; cache reads cost roughly 10% of base input price and are faster, while writes cost about 25% more. A large reused coding standard and tool-definition block is the canonical caching candidate for a high-volume pipeline.

question: Your team is splitting one overloaded "dev-helper" subagent into several. Which signals indicate you should define distinct custom agent types rather than one general agent? (Select all that apply.)
options: The roles have clearly separable responsibilities you can verify independently
They all share the exact same prompt, tools, and success criteria
One role is high-volume and cheap while another is rare and hard
The roles need different tool permissions and guardrails
explanation: You split into specialized agent types when roles differ in permissions/guardrails, have separable independently-verifiable responsibilities, or have different cost/difficulty profiles that warrant different models. If everything is identical, splitting adds complexity for no benefit.

question: A PostToolUse hook is configured to auto-format any file Claude writes. Architecturally, what is the role of a hook in Claude Code?
options: A remote MCP server reached over HTTP
A model-invoked capability the agent calls like a tool
A persistent memory file loaded into context
A shell command fired on a lifecycle event that can observe, block, or modify tool calls
explanation: Hooks are shell commands fired on lifecycle events such as PreToolUse and PostToolUse; they can observe, block, or modify tool calls and are used for guardrails, formatting, and policy. Auto-formatting written files on PostToolUse is a textbook hook use.

question: You expose a read-only company knowledge base to Claude through MCP. The host app, not the model, should decide which documents to include in context. Which primitive matches this?
options: Resources
Hooks
Prompts
Tools
explanation: Resources are application-controlled context/data exposed by URI that the host app chooses to include, which is exactly a host-curated read-only knowledge base. Tools are model-controlled and Prompts are user-controlled, so neither fits "the host decides what to include."

question: A code-review agent in CI keeps looping: it edits a file, re-reads it, finds a nit, edits again, indefinitely. Which mitigation most directly prevents this failure mode?
options: Using cache_control on the system prompt
A max-iteration cap on the agentic loop
Increasing the context window to 1M tokens
Switching the prompt to XML tags
explanation: Infinite loops are a named failure mode of the agentic loop, and the listed mitigation is a max-iteration cap. A larger context window or caching addresses cost and exhaustion but neither bounds the number of iterations.

question: For a forced single-call extraction step in a batch pipeline, you must guarantee the model calls your "extract_fields" tool and no other. Which setting achieves this?
options: tool_choice set to "none"
tool_choice set to "auto"
tool_choice set to force that specific tool
Omitting the tools array
explanation: tool_choice can be auto (model decides), any (must call some tool), tool (force a specific tool), or none. To guarantee a specific tool is called, you force that tool via the "tool" option.

question: A governance policy requires that a specific compliance-checklist workflow is launched the same way by every engineer, on demand, as a slash command. Which Claude Code/MCP construct best packages this?
options: A PreToolUse hook
A higher temperature setting
An MCP Resource
An MCP Prompt (user-controlled template surfaced as a slash command)
explanation: MCP Prompts are user-controlled reusable templates that can be surfaced as slash commands, which standardizes an on-demand workflow every engineer triggers the same way. Resources are host-chosen context and hooks fire automatically, so neither matches a user-invoked checklist.

question: In a long-running orchestrator session, the conversation is approaching the context-window limit and earlier results are being forgotten. What is the standard technique?
options: Compaction / summarization of earlier context
Adding more few-shot examples
Lowering max_tokens on each response
Setting tool_choice to "any"
explanation: The context window is finite, and long agentic sessions exhaust it; the standard response is compaction/summarization that condenses earlier context. The other options either ignore context size or make it worse.

question: You are writing the system prompt for an autonomous fleet supervisor agent. According to the brief, where should the most important guardrail instructions be placed within a long prompt?
options: Only in a footnote after the examples
At the start and end of the context, with clear structure
Randomly distributed to maximize coverage
Buried in the middle so the model reads everything first
explanation: Because of the "lost in the middle" effect, models attend less to information buried in the middle of a long context, so critical instructions and data belong at the start and end, reinforced with structure. This is a core prompt-engineering and context-management principle.

question: A team wants Claude Code to auto-trigger a packaged "generate-release-notes" workflow that the model can invoke when appropriate, and engineers can also run via /generate-release-notes. Which construct fits?
options: A streaming SSE connection
A stop_sequences entry
A Skill in .claude/skills/
A nested CLAUDE.md
explanation: Skills under .claude/skills/ are packaged, model-invoked capabilities, and many are also user-invocable via /skill-name. That dual model-and-user invocation matches a release-notes workflow exactly.

question: To keep a multi-agent code-migration system from producing inconsistent results across agents, you want to enforce one set of coding invariants everywhere. Which approaches enforce systemic rules centrally? (Select all that apply.)
options: A shared system prompt section all agents inherit
Hard rules in CLAUDE.md
Verbally reminding each agent author to be careful
Hooks and permission policy that block violations
explanation: Systemic invariants should be enforced centrally via the system prompt, hooks, CLAUDE.md hard rules, and permission policy, not by hoping each agent behaves. Verbal reminders to authors are exactly the non-systemic approach the brief warns against.

question: In a headless CI run, you must force Claude Code to use only an explicitly allowed set of commands and tools. Which mechanism governs this?
options: The stop_sequences array
The temperature parameter
A prefill on the assistant turn
settings.json with permission modes
explanation: Claude Code uses settings.json together with permission modes to control allowed tools and commands. In a locked-down CI run, that is the mechanism for restricting the agent to an approved set.

question: An orchestrator must process a stream of incoming items where each item flows through fixed stages and there is no need to wait for all items before moving on. Which orchestration pattern is this?
options: Pipeline (a stage per item, no barrier)
Judge panel
Loop-until-dry
Parallel fan-out with a barrier
explanation: A pipeline processes each item through stages with no barrier, so items flow independently. Fan-out with a barrier is used when you must collect all results before proceeding, which is explicitly not required here.

question: You build an MCP server for a remote SaaS that many distributed agents connect to over the network. Which transport is appropriate?
options: A PostToolUse hook
Streamable HTTP/SSE
stdio (local subprocess)
A CLAUDE.md import
explanation: MCP transports are stdio for local subprocesses and streamable HTTP/SSE for remote servers. A remote SaaS reached over the network uses the HTTP/SSE transport.

question: A judge-panel orchestration scores generated answers, but reviewers complain the scores swing run to run. Which change most improves consistency of the judge outputs?
options: Remove the rubric and let judges decide freely
Raise the judges' temperature for more diverse opinions
Give the judges few-shot examples and a structured rubric with low temperature
Have one judge instead of a panel
explanation: Few-shot examples drive output consistency and format adherence, and a structured rubric with low temperature constrains judge variability. The other options increase variance or abandon the panel rather than stabilizing the scoring.

question: A system prompt for an agent fleet must specify role, instructions, constraints, and exact output format. According to the brief, what is the guiding principle for writing it?
options: Use only emojis to save tokens
Keep it vague so the model has freedom
Rely on the model to infer the format from context
Be explicit: clearly state role, instructions, constraints, and specify the format
explanation: The brief instructs writing a clear system prompt that states role, instructions, and constraints, and to be explicit and specify format. Vagueness or relying on inference undermines the consistency a production fleet needs.

question: Across a fleet of agents, you want to standardize how complex inputs (context, examples, instructions) are delimited so Claude parses them reliably. Which technique does the brief recommend?
options: Structure inputs with XML tags such as <context> and <example>
Concatenate everything into one unlabeled paragraph
Use only JSON with no surrounding instructions
Encode the instructions as base64
explanation: The brief recommends XML tags such as <context> and <example> to structure inputs and sections well for Claude. Clear delimiting helps the model attend to the right parts, especially in long fleet prompts.

question: A platform team is deciding the default model for thousands of narrow, high-volume classifier subagents in a fleet. Which choice best balances cost and fitness?
options: Sonnet for the orchestrator and Opus for every classifier
Haiku, because the roles are narrow, high-volume, and latency/cost-sensitive
Always pick the model with the largest context window
Opus for every subagent to maximize quality
explanation: Haiku is the fastest and cheapest family, recommended for high-volume, low-latency, cheap tasks like narrow classifiers. Reserving Opus for the hardest reasoning and using Haiku for narrow subagents is the cost-aware fleet choice.

question: You add a critic step after a generation agent to catch errors before output ships. In agentic terms, what failure mode does an explicit verification/critic step most directly mitigate?
options: Cache TTL expiry
stdio transport limits
Shipping incorrect results due to no verification (and premature stopping)
CLAUDE.md hierarchy conflicts
explanation: Verification/critic steps are a listed mitigation that catch errors and counter premature stopping in the agentic loop. The other options name unrelated transport, caching, or config concerns.

question: A loop-until-dry queue-draining orchestrator needs a stopping rule. Which rule matches that pattern's definition?
options: Stop when the cache expires
Stop after the first tool call
Never stop; run forever
Stop after K consecutive empty rounds
explanation: The loop-until-dry pattern repeats until K empty rounds, meaning the queue has been drained. A fixed empty-round threshold is the stopping criterion that defines this pattern.

question: During a CI review job you observe runaway token cost from an agent that keeps re-exploring the codebase. Besides a max-iteration cap, which controls are recommended to bound cost? (Select all that apply.)
options: Token/cost budgets per run
Explicit success criteria so the agent knows when it is done
Setting temperature to its maximum
Timeouts on tool calls and the overall session
explanation: Mitigations for runaway token cost include max-iteration caps, token/cost budgets, timeouts, and explicit success criteria. Maximizing temperature does nothing to bound cost and tends to increase erratic behavior.

question: You expose three overlapping search tools to a fleet: search_docs, find_documents, and lookup_files, all with similar descriptions. Agents keep picking the wrong one. What is the best fix?
options: Consolidate to a small, distinct tool set with sharp, non-overlapping descriptions
Raise temperature so the model explores tools
Add a fourth similar tool to give more choices
Hide the descriptions to force the model to guess
explanation: Overlapping or vague descriptions cause wrong-tool selection; the remedy is a small, distinct tool set with descriptions stating what each does and when to use or avoid it. Adding more similar tools or removing descriptions makes selection worse.

question: A compliance officer wants every Claude Code agent action logged for audit without blocking work. Which mechanism cleanly implements passive observation of tool activity?
options: A larger context window
A hook that observes tool calls (e.g., PostToolUse) and emits audit logs
Increasing max_tokens
A prefill on each assistant message
explanation: Hooks fire on lifecycle events such as PostToolUse and can observe tool calls; configured to log rather than block, they provide passive audit trails. This is a standard governance use of hooks across a fleet.

question: For a tool that returns very long documents, an architect worries the most important data gets buried mid-context and ignored. Which single design response best aligns with the brief?
options: Increase temperature so the model scans more thoroughly
Pad the middle with filler so important data is surrounded
Place the most critical instructions and data at the start and end of context
Always rely on the model to find buried details
explanation: Because of the "lost in the middle" effect, critical instructions and data should be placed at the start and end of the context, reinforced with structure. Padding the middle, trusting the model to find buried details, or changing temperature do not address the attention profile.

question: A subagent must return its result so the orchestrator can decide next steps, but you do not want the subagent's chain-of-thought polluting the orchestrator's context. Which property of the Agent tool helps here?
options: The subagent edits the orchestrator's system prompt
The subagent streams its reasoning to the user
The subagent shares the orchestrator's full context buffer
The subagent runs in its own isolated context and returns only its final message
explanation: Subagents spawned via the Agent tool run with their own isolated context and return only their final message to the caller. That isolation is precisely what keeps the orchestrator's context clean of the subagent's intermediate reasoning.

question: In a GitHub Actions integration, you want Claude Code to comment on a PR only when it finds a real issue, and exit silently otherwise. Which combination best implements this deterministically? (Select all that apply.)
options: Run headless with -p and have it emit schema-constrained output indicating issue/no-issue
Gate the comment step in the workflow on the parsed result
Rely on reading prose to decide whether to comment
Force the model to always comment regardless of findings
explanation: Headless mode emits output the workflow can parse, and forcing structured output via a schema gives a reliable issue/no-issue flag the next step can gate on. Relying on prose or always commenting undermines the deterministic, conditional behavior required.

question: You are choosing a model for the orchestrator agent that must do the hardest planning and decomposition in a large multi-agent system. Which family is most appropriate?
options: Opus
A non-Claude model to save cost
Whichever has the smallest context window
Haiku
explanation: The orchestrator does the hardest reasoning, decomposition, and agentic work, which is what Opus is for as the most capable family. Narrow subagents can use cheaper models, but the planning brain should be the strongest.

question: A prompt must produce a JSON object that downstream code parses, and occasional malformed output is breaking the pipeline. Beyond tool-use schemas, which prompting techniques further improve format adherence? (Select all that apply.)
options: Provide few-shot examples of the exact desired output
Validate the output against a schema and reject/repair on failure
Ask the model to write a poem first to warm up
Prefill the assistant turn to start the JSON structure
explanation: Format adherence is improved by few-shot examples, prefilling the assistant turn, and validating against a schema (with repair on failure), in addition to tool-use input_schema. An irrelevant warm-up step does nothing for reliability.

question: An architect proposes one giant MCP server exposing tools for billing, HR, search, and deployment. What does the brief recommend instead?
options: Expose everything as Resources to avoid tools
One server per logical capability
One server per individual user
Merge all capabilities to minimize server count
explanation: MCP design guidance is one server per logical capability, with clear tool names, resources for read context, and prompts for user-triggered workflows. Cramming unrelated domains into one server muddies boundaries and tool selection.

question: A reasoning-heavy agent must work through a multi-step compliance decision, and you want it to reason explicitly in its output before committing to an answer using only prompt wording. Which single technique does this?
options: Setting max_tokens to 1
Lowering temperature to 0
Chain-of-thought prompting ("think step by step")
Forbidding any intermediate reasoning
explanation: Chain-of-thought prompting ("think step by step") is the prompt-wording technique that elicits explicit reasoning before the answer; extended thinking is the model-mode alternative. Forcing one token or forbidding reasoning prevents the behavior, and temperature alone does not elicit reasoning steps.

question: A fleet's cache hit rate is low because each call slightly varies the very first lines of an otherwise-identical system prompt. What is the fix?
options: Vary the prefix more so the cache refreshes
Move variation to the system field's first line
Disable caching entirely
Keep the cached prefix byte-stable and put per-call variation after the cache breakpoint
explanation: Prompt caching matches a stable prefix up to a cache_control breakpoint; any variation in that prefix invalidates the cache. Keeping the prefix byte-stable and pushing per-call differences after the breakpoint restores high hit rates.

question: You must enforce that no agent in the fleet can call a destructive "delete_customer" tool unless a human approves. Which layered controls implement this governance? (Select all that apply.)
options: A PreToolUse hook that blocks or requires approval for that tool
Permission policy in settings.json restricting the tool
Hoping the system prompt phrasing discourages it
A hard rule in CLAUDE.md documenting the constraint
explanation: Destructive-action governance layers a PreToolUse hook (block/approve), permission policy in settings.json, and a CLAUDE.md hard rule, enforcing the invariant centrally rather than via prompt phrasing alone. Relying on hope is exactly the non-systemic anti-pattern.

question: An agent occasionally hits rate limits during peak CI load and fails the job. Which client-side reliability practice does the brief recommend?
options: Retries with backoff and proper rate-limit handling
Switching to the largest model to avoid limits
Sending all requests simultaneously to finish faster
Removing error handling to surface failures immediately
explanation: Reliability practices include retries with backoff, idempotency, and handling rate limits gracefully. Bursting requests or removing error handling makes rate-limit failures worse.

question: A custom agent type is registered to handle database migrations and must only ever touch migration files and the schema. What is the cleanest way to constrain it?
options: Add a sentence to the global CLAUDE.md asking it to be careful
Scope its tool permissions narrowly via settings/permission policy for that agent
Increase its context window
Give it Opus so it makes fewer mistakes
explanation: Custom agent types should be confined via scoped tool permissions and permission policy so they can only act within their remit. Prompts, model choice, and context size do not enforce a hard scope boundary.

question: In the Messages API, which field carries the prior user and assistant turns that give the model conversational context?
options: tool_choice
The system field
The messages array
stop_sequences
explanation: The Messages API uses a system prompt plus a messages array of user/assistant turns, with max_tokens, temperature, and stop_sequences as controls. Conversational context lives in the messages array.

question: A team wants Claude to constrain its output to begin with a specific JSON opening so a streaming parser can start immediately. Which technique does this?
options: Use tool_choice "none"
Set temperature to 0 only
Add the JSON to stop_sequences
Prefill the assistant turn with the opening of the JSON
explanation: Prefill seeds the start of the assistant turn to constrain output, such as beginning a JSON object. This lets a streaming parser begin on a known structure immediately.

question: An architect must decide between fan-out-with-barrier and pipeline for a job that aggregates findings from 12 independent analyzers into a single report. Which fits and why?
options: Fan-out with a barrier, because the report needs all 12 results before it can be produced
Loop-until-dry, because you repeat until empty
Pipeline, because each analyzer can finish independently with no aggregation
Judge panel, because you are scoring outputs
explanation: When a final artifact requires all parallel results, you use parallel fan-out with a barrier that waits for every analyzer before aggregating. A barrier-free pipeline suits independent per-item flow, which does not fit a single combined report.

question: You are reviewing an MCP server design where the model is expected to "read" reference policy documents but the documents change per request and the host should pick which to attach. Which mismatch should you flag?
options: The policies should be Prompts, since the host picks them
The policies should be Resources (host-controlled), not Tools
The server should be merged with the deployment server
Nothing; reading documents is always a Tool
explanation: When the host application chooses which read-only context to attach by URI, that is a Resource, not a model-invoked Tool. Modeling host-curated documents as Tools is the design mismatch to flag.

question: To reduce cost across a fleet that re-sends the same large tool-definition block on every call, where should the cache breakpoint go relative to the tool definitions?
options: Before the tool definitions so they are excluded
In the middle of one tool's schema
After the stable tool definitions so they are cached and reused
Caching cannot include tool definitions
explanation: Large reused tool definitions are a prime prompt-caching target; the cache_control breakpoint goes after the stable block so it is written once and read cheaply thereafter. Excluding the definitions or splitting a schema undermines the cache.

question: A governance team wants a hook to reject any tool call whose arguments include a production database URL during business-as-usual runs. At which lifecycle event should the hook fire to block before execution?
options: PostToolUse
On model load
On session end only
PreToolUse
explanation: To block a tool call before it executes, the hook must fire on PreToolUse; PostToolUse runs after the action has already occurred. Blocking dangerous arguments requires the pre-execution event.

question: A fleet supervisor agent quits as soon as the first tool call returns, before the goal is actually met. Which named agentic-loop failure mode is this?
options: Premature stopping
Infinite loop
Error cascade
Context exhaustion
explanation: Premature stopping is a named failure mode of the agentic loop where the agent quits before the success criteria are satisfied. It is the opposite of infinite looping, and distinct from context exhaustion and error cascades.

question: In an error-cascade scenario, one subagent returns a malformed result and downstream subagents compound the error. Which design choices reduce error cascades? (Select all that apply.)
options: Make tools idempotent and add timeouts
Validate subagent outputs against a schema before passing them downstream
Add a verification/critic step between stages
Remove all max-iteration caps to let agents self-correct forever
explanation: Error cascades are reduced by validating outputs against a schema, inserting verification/critic steps, and using idempotent tools with timeouts to contain failures. Removing iteration caps invites infinite loops rather than preventing cascades.

question: A team asks whether to model a "run_report" capability that the model should autonomously invoke as an MCP Resource. What is the correct guidance?
options: It cannot be modeled in MCP
Model it as a Tool, because the model autonomously invokes the action
Model it as a Resource, because it returns data
Model it as a Prompt, because it is reusable
explanation: An action the model decides to invoke autonomously is a Tool in MCP; Resources are application-controlled read context the model does not invoke. "run_report" being model-invoked makes it a Tool regardless of the data it returns.

question: A nested service-level CLAUDE.md says "use 2-space indent" while the project root says "use 4-space indent." For files in that service, which wins and why?
options: Neither; conflicting rules cancel out
The root file (4-space), because root always wins
The nested file (2-space), because deeper/more-specific files extend and can override higher ones
Whichever was edited most recently
explanation: CLAUDE.md is hierarchical and deeper, more-specific files extend and can override higher ones. For files inside the service, the nested CLAUDE.md's 2-space rule takes precedence over the root's 4-space rule.

question: A headless agent in CI must produce deterministic, reproducible output for a snapshot test. Which parameter setting most reduces output variability?
options: A high temperature
A longer stop_sequences list
A larger max_tokens
A low temperature (e.g., 0)
explanation: Temperature controls sampling randomness; setting it low (near 0) minimizes variability, which is what a reproducible snapshot test needs. max_tokens and stop_sequences affect length and stopping, not determinism of content.

question: To improve a complex multi-step reasoning task's accuracy without changing the model, which prompt-engineering technique is recommended?
options: Chain-of-thought: instruct the model to think step by step
Force a single-token answer
Randomize the order of sections each call
Remove all instructions to keep the prompt short
explanation: For complex reasoning the brief recommends chain-of-thought ("think step by step"), or extended thinking. Stripping instructions, forcing a single token, or randomizing sections all degrade rather than improve reasoning.

question: A governance review asks how to reuse the same large analysis prompt across an interactive tool and a nightly batch job cost-effectively. Which two capabilities apply, respectively? (Select all that apply.)
options: stop_sequences to lower cost in both
Higher temperature to reduce tokens
The Message Batches API for the nightly non-real-time job
Prompt caching for the interactive tool's repeated large prefix
explanation: Prompt caching addresses the interactive tool's repeated large prefix (cheaper, faster reads), while the Message Batches API handles the nightly non-real-time job asynchronously at roughly a 50% discount. stop_sequences and temperature do not reduce cost.

question: A governance team standardizes prompts across many agents and wants the most reliable way to guarantee a parseable status object from each agent's final turn. Which technique is the most reliable per the brief?
options: Ask politely for JSON in plain prose
Force structured output via tool use with a defined input_schema
Raise temperature to encourage formatting
Use stop_sequences to mark the end of JSON
explanation: The brief states structured outputs are most reliably forced via tool use with an input_schema, optionally combined with prefill and validation. For a fleet that must emit a parseable status object, schema-constrained tool use is the canonical choice.

question: An architect is consolidating an MCP server's surface and asks how to organize capabilities so agents select correctly. Which design choices follow the brief's MCP guidance? (Select all that apply.)
options: Give tools clear, distinct names and non-overlapping descriptions
Expose read-only context as Resources and user-triggered workflows as Prompts
Maximize the number of overlapping tools to give the model options
Keep one server per logical capability
explanation: MCP design guidance is one server per logical capability, clear and distinct tool names with sharp descriptions, Resources for read context, and Prompts for user-triggered workflows. Piling on overlapping tools degrades selection accuracy.
